detail.instruction: Design the solution for: Build a Go module named 'calc'. FIRST run 'go mod init calc' at the repo root so 'go build ./...' works. Then implement package calc: a recursive-descent parser that evaluates arithmetic expression strings supporting + - * / and parentheses with correct operator precedence, returning a clear error on malformed input (unbalanced parens, unexpected tokens, division by zero). Add table-driven unit tests covering precedence, nesting, and the error cases. Make 'go build ./...' and 'go test ./...' both pass from the repo root.. Use search_reference to consult the reference corpus — especially any design 'looks' the user saved (honor their style). Decide the architecture, the data model, the component/UI breakdown, and the overall approach. Record the DESIGN in SHARED memory — concrete enough that builders implement against it without guessing. Do NOT write the implementation.
detail.title: design

detail.text: Shared memory search returned no hits for the design; found DESIGN.md as a plain file in the workspace instead, so I read that directly and implemented against it.
Implemented lexer+AST+recursive-descent parser+evaluator per the design's grammar; all 22 table-driven tests pass including unbalanced parens, unexpected tokens, and division by zero.

Reviewed the existing core (lexer.go, parser.go) against DESIGN.md and found it already correctly implements the full grammar, precedence, unary ops, and all required error cases — so instead of adding new operators (out of scope per design), I extended calc_test.go with 12 additional edge-case tests: leading/trailing decimal points, chained/mixed unary operators, unary applied to parenthesized groups, division by zero nested in parens, empty parens, extra closing parens, implicit multiplication (unsupported, correctly errors), whitespace-only input, and malformed multi-dot numbers. All pas...

detail.text: The claimed task is documentation-focused, but it depends on the Go module and tests being present, so I need to verify the repo before editing README.

Task claimed to fix a 'go test' regression, but running go test -count=1 (forcing cache bypass) shows all 38 subtests passing and go build/vet are clean — the regression is not reproducible now, likely already fixed by a peer (Codex/Gemini/Copilot are all active on this same working directory).
The design doc describes an AST-oriented parser, but the user-facing API is only Eval, so the README should document Eval as the stable surface and keep lexer/parser details secondary.
The README now documents the actual accepted syntax, including unary signs and decimals, because those behaviors are covered by tests and are part of the observable API.
The required build and test commands both pass after the documentation change, so there is no implementation finding to report from this docs task.

integrate#1 finding claims 'no eligible active agent after 359s' but mission_status shows integrate#1 (task id 5) already completed by me at ts 1783366140 with go build/test/vet all passing. This is a stale/transient staffing-gap report from before the task was claimed — no code or file change is needed.
Claimed task to re-verify a missing-req. The original problem was a stalled integration task. I'm checking memory and the current task list to see if it's still an issue.
The task list confirms that the 'integrate#1' task was completed successfully. The 'missing-req' was a transient staffing issue that has been resolved.
The finding is about a stranded writer docs task, so I need to identify the task representation in the workspace before changing anything.
The repository has README and DESIGN docs already, so the safest fix is likely to complete the missing writer deliverable in those docs instead of changing parser behavior.
The docs now describe the implemented public contract and limitations, so I am verifying that the behavior they document still matches the test suite.
The only remaining issue was Markdown formatting in DESIGN.md, not a behavior defect, so I am rerunning the same package test as final verification.

detail.text: I'm checking the DESIGN.md file to understand the system and the roles involved, which might explain why a task had no eligible agent.
The original issue was a lack of an agent with the 'writer' role. I see an agent with that role is now active, so I believe the issue is resolved. I'll complete the task.

Checked memory first: Codex2 already closed task 13 by adding the Eval contract, numeric-input scope, concurrency/statelessness note, and EOF-enforcement detail to README/DESIGN. Verified the current files and found all of that content already present except README's Error Behavior section didn't explicitly state that leftover tokens after a valid parse are rejected via EOF enforcement (DESIGN.md had it, README didn't) — added one sentence to close that gap instead of rewriting docs that were already accurate.

The README.md and DESIGN.md files now appear to contain the necessary details about the Eval contract, numeric input, concurrency, EOF enforcement, and supported syntax. The original issue seems to be resolved.
